rationale: The cited paper is used repeatedly as more than background: it is invoked to support key premises about negative information being especially diagnostic and to position prior work on scandal effects focused on the scandalized brand itself. In the discussion, the authors explicitly frame their contribution as moving beyond work such as Ahluwalia et al. (2000), and they link their denial logic to the cited paper's counterargumentation response idea. These uses indicate a meaningful conceptual role in framing hypotheses and interpreting contribution, not a passing mention.
relation_description: The citing paper extends Ahluwalia et al. (2000) from within-brand negative-publicity effects to spillover across product categories and competing brands. It also builds on that prior response-strategy logic by examining when competitor denials correct spillover versus boomerang.
